column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Inconel Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/metal/specialty/inconel-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Inconel oxide layer removal
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Inconel absorption and minimal substrate damage
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control
Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage
Minimum fluence required for effective Inconel oxide removal
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient oxide layer ablation
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning
Number of cleaning passes for complete oxide removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

Typical range for industrial pulsed fiber lasers cleaning metals like Inconel; starting at 100 W balances efficiency and safety to avoid melting (Inconel melting point ~1300-1400°C); max safe ~300-500 W with proper scanning to prevent substrate damage. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and study in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on Ni-based alloys.

Effective cleaning at 1000-3000 mm/s for light contamination (e.g., oils), slower (500-1500 mm/s) for heavy oxides; 2000 mm/s recommended for optimal balance on Inconel to minimize heat input given its high thermal conductivity (~15 W/m·K). Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals and Journal of Cleaner Production (2020) case study on superalloys.

Nanosecond regime (10-200 ns) standard for 1064 nm cleaning of reflective metals like Inconel to enable ablation without excessive plasma shielding; 50 ns recommended for minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm), as picosecond/femtosecond better for ultra-precision but costlier. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019) on pulse duration effects for Ni alloys.

20-100 kHz typical for controlled energy delivery in cleaning; 100 kHz recommended for Inconel to achieve fast processing while limiting heat accumulation (trade-off: higher rates speed up cleaning but risk thermal buildup on low-reflectivity contaminants). Source: Academic literature in Journal of Laser Applications (2021) reviewing rep rates for metal surface preparation.

Fluence range for contaminant removal without damaging Inconel (ablation threshold ~2-4 J/cm² due to high reflectivity ~60% at 1064 nm); 1.5 J/cm² safe for cleaning, below damage threshold of ~5 J/cm². Source: Ablation threshold studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (2017) on Inconel 718.

50-300 μm for fine detail cleaning, up to 500 μm for large areas; 200 μm recommended for Inconel to ensure uniform fluence distribution and avoid hotspots on irregular surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems.

Single pass for light contamination, 2-3 for moderate/heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on Inconel without excessive wear, allowing residue assessment between passes. Source: Industry practice in Aerospace Manufacturing and case studies from Laser Cleaning International (2022).

30-50% for speed on large areas, 60-80% for uniform coverage; 60% recommended to ensure no gaps in cleaning Inconel surfaces while optimizing process time. Source: Process optimization studies in Optics and Laser Technology (2020) on scan strategies for alloys.
